metadata.dataset_name: Situational Rights Taxonomy (SRT) v1.0 - India Police Encounters
landmark_cases: D.K. Basu v State of WB (1997)
Lalita Kumari v Govt of UP (2014)
Prakash Singh v Union of India (2006)
K.S. Puttaswamy v Union of India (2017)
Arnesh Kumar v State of Bihar (2014)
metadata.disclaimer: General legal information only. All entries require advocate review before use.
metadata.project: The Law in Your Language - NyayaCard

entries.situation_description: Traffic officer asking you to unlock your phone or show DigiLocker
entries.right_plain: You are NOT required to unlock your phone or show DigiLocker. You must only produce your driving license, RC, and insurance certificate.
entries.script: Officer, under Section 129 of the Motor Vehicles Act, I am only required to produce my license, RC, and insurance. I am happy to show these documents. I am not required to unlock my personal device.
escalation: Ask for officer name and badge number
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not physically resist. Do not refuse to show license/RC/insurance.

entries.situation_description: Officer issuing a wrong challan or demanding cash payment
entries.right_plain: You have the right to receive a proper e-challan. Officers cannot demand cash on the spot. You can contest any challan within 60 days.
entries.script: Officer, I request a proper e-challan with details of the violation. I will pay through the official portal. I am not required to pay cash on the spot.
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not pay cash to the officer. Do not bribe.

entries.situation_description: Police attempting to arrest you without showing a warrant
entries.right_plain: Police can only arrest without warrant for cognizable offences. They MUST tell you WHY and inform your family. For offences under 7 years punishment, they should issue a notice first.
entries.script: Officer, please tell me the grounds for my arrest. Under Section 47 of the BNSS, I have the right to know why I am being arrested. Please inform my family member at [number] as required under Section 48.
escalation: Ask for grounds of arrest (S.47 BNSS)
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not physically resist. Do not sign blank papers. Do not make statements without lawyer.

entries.situation_description: You are at the police station or in custody after arrest
entries.right_plain: Rights: (1) know why arrested, (2) meet lawyer during interrogation, (3) family informed, (4) Magistrate within 24 hours, (5) no torture, (6) free legal aid if needed.
entries.right_formal: BNSS 2023 - Sections 36, 38, 47, 48, 57; Constitution Art.21, 22; D.K. Basu guidelines
entries.script: I want to exercise my right under Section 38 of the BNSS to meet my lawyer. Please prepare the arrest memo under Section 36 and inform my family under Section 48. I will not make any statement until I speak to my lawyer.
escalation: Call NALSA helpline 15100 for free legal aid
If not before Magistrate in 24hrs file habeas corpus
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not confess under pressure. Do not sign blank papers. Do not bribe.
source_statutes: D.K. Basu v State of WB 1997

entries.situation_description: A woman being arrested or detained by police
entries.right_plain: A woman CANNOT be arrested between sunset and sunrise except with JMFC permission. Only a woman officer can arrest. No male officer can touch a woman during arrest.
entries.script: I am a woman and under Section 43 of the BNSS, you cannot arrest me at night without Judicial Magistrate permission. I have the right to be arrested only by a woman police officer. No male officer may touch me.
escalation: Ask for JMFC permission order for night arrest
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not go with male officers alone. Do not physically resist.

entries.situation_description: Police at your door wanting to enter and search your home
entries.right_plain: Police CANNOT enter without a search warrant from a Magistrate, except in emergencies. Even with warrant, search needs two independent witnesses from the locality.
entries.script: Officer, please show me a valid search warrant from a Magistrate. Under the BNSS, you need a warrant to enter my home. I request the search be conducted with two independent witnesses from my neighbourhood as required by law.
escalation: File complaint if illegal (SP or Art.226)
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not physically block police. Do not destroy evidence. Do not sign panchnama without reading.

entries.situation_description: You want to record police or they are stopping you / deleting your footage
entries.right_plain: You can record police performing duties in public under Article 19(1)(a). Police cannot seize or delete recordings without warrant. Bombay HC ruled filming in police stations is not an offence.
entries.right_formal: Constitution Art.19(1)(a); Kerala Police Act 2011 S.33(2); Puttaswamy 2017
entries.script: I am recording this interaction peacefully from a safe distance under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution. I am not interfering with your duty. You cannot seize my device or delete recordings without a warrant.
escalation: If device seized note officer details
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not obstruct police while recording. Do not push camera in faces. Do not record in restricted areas.

entries.situation_description: Police stopping you and demanding identification without specific reason
entries.right_plain: No general law requires carrying ID at all times. Police cannot detain you solely for not having ID. But refusing to give your NAME can lead to arrest under Section 39 BNSS.
entries.script: Officer, I am happy to tell you my name and address. May I ask what offence I am suspected of? I would like to know the reason for being stopped.
escalation: Give name and address (avoid S.39 issues)
If not under arrest ask if free to leave
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not refuse to give your name. Do not be aggressive. Do not run.

entries.situation_description: Police refusing to register your FIR (First Information Report)
entries.right_plain: Police are LEGALLY BOUND to register FIR for cognizable offences. Refusal is punishable. You can send complaint to SP by post, approach Magistrate, or file online.
entries.right_formal: BNSS 2023 - Section 173; Lalita Kumari v Govt of UP (2014)
entries.script: Officer, under Section 173 of the BNSS and the Supreme Court ruling in Lalita Kumari, you are legally required to register my FIR. Refusal is itself punishable. Please register my complaint now.
escalation: Written complaint by registered post to SP
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not bribe police. Do not file false FIR. Do not threaten.
source_statutes: Lalita Kumari v Govt of UP 2014

entries.situation_description: Filing a complaint against police misconduct (abuse, corruption, illegal detention)
entries.right_plain: Multiple channels: Police Complaints Authority, NHRC, State Human Rights Commission, SP Office. For serious abuse approach High Court. Compensation available for groundless arrest.
entries.right_formal: Prakash Singh v Union of India (2006); NHRC Act 1993; BNSS 2023 S.399; Constitution Art.226
entries.script: I wish to file a formal complaint regarding police misconduct. I am documenting the officer details and incident. I will file with the NHRC / State Human Rights Commission / SP Office.
escalation: Written complaint to SP by registered post
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not file false complaints. Do not confront officer directly. Do not delay.
source_statutes: Prakash Singh v Union of India 2006

entries.situation_description: Police demanding to search your phone, messages, or social media
entries.right_plain: Police CANNOT search your phone without consent or court order. Phone is protected under right to privacy (Art.21). Even during arrest, physical search does not cover digital data extraction.
entries.right_formal: Constitution Art.21; Puttaswamy 2017; BNSS 2023 S.49; IT Act 2000 S.69
entries.script: Officer, my phone is protected under the right to privacy (Puttaswamy, Supreme Court). You need a court order under Section 69 of the IT Act to search my phone. I do not consent to unlocking my device.
entries.what_not_to_do: Do not hand over unlocked phone. Do not delete data in front of officers. Do not physically resist.
